data.title: Davenport Fixed Income Market Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Q4 2025 Market Review

Fixed income markets closed 2025 on a high note. Reliable coupon payments remained the dominant driver throughout the fourth quarter. Bond prices also continued to benefit from robust investor demand in both the primary and secondary markets. Bouts of short-lived volatility proved temporary with economic data pointing to a stable labor market and an economy that ultimately trumped geopolitical concerns and Federal Reserve independence questions.

The U.S. Treasury bond market’s performance illustrated the strength of America’s economy. The ten-year yield finished the fourth quarter slightly above 4.1%. The importance of this particular tenor on the risk-free yield curve cannot be understated. An elevated ten-year yield often means strong economic growth, while a depressed ten-year yield indicates economic weakening. We believe the current yield reflects a durable economy that bodes well for lenders. Turning to the front-end of the U.S. Treasury curve, these yields are generally more correlated to the Federal Reserve’s dual mandate (price stability & maximum employment). The two-year yield ended the year at roughly 3.5%, well above the zero interest rate policy (ZIRP) days.

Investment grade corporate bonds continued to highlight robust investor demand for additional yield. At the company level, strong corporate fundamentals remained impressive. The conundrum is bond valuations are stretched, approaching multi-decade richness. We do believe spreads (yield compensation over the risk-free rate) will return to palatable valuations as mean reversion typically wins out. That said, money markets hold nearly $8 trillion in available cash that could fund ongoing corporate debt purchases at elevated prices.

The Davenport Asset Management Fixed Income team manages 5 models with oversight by the Investment Policy Committee (IPC). The team takes a dynamic approach to managing interest rate and credit risk with a conservative focus designed to meet individual client needs.

The new year will undoubtedly deliver its share of twists and turns. With the sun setting on the Powell & Co. Federal Reserve era, President Trump’s push for lower interest rates has raised the question of Fed independence. Shortened yet dynamic geopolitical narratives look to continue to fuel market volatility. In addition to these macro level developments, we are particularly focused on two trends in 2026 – fiscal and monetary policy impact on the U.S. Treasury bond market and whether more rational compensation will prevail in the corporate bond market.

Sincerely,

Kevin J. Hopkins Jr., CFA, Lead Fixed Income Portfolio Manager

data.title: First Horizon: Undervalued With Dividends And Robust Share Buybacks
data.description:
Regional banks remain a good value in today’s market, especially as many remain meaningfully undervalued compared to megabanks like Bank of America Corporation (BAC) and JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM). Regional banks have a smaller asset base than large banks, meaning that it doesn’t take as much growth to move their bottom lines.

This brings me to First Horizon Corporation (FHN), which currently sports an attractive P/E multiple with solid business results. At the current price of $24.12, FHN carries a forward P/E of 11.35 and pays a well-covered 2.5% dividend yield, as shown below.

In this article, I'm highlighting FHN, including recent business results, and discussing what makes it an attractive value and income play for potentially strong total returns, so let’s get started!

Why FHN?

First Horizon Corporation is a leading regional financial services company that’s headquartered in Memphis, Tennessee, with a legacy dating back to 1864. It serves individual consumers, businesses, and institutions through its banking subsidiary, First Horizon Bank, across the southeastern U.S. At present, FHN has over 400 banking centers and manages over $80 billion in total assets.

In addition to traditional banking services, FHN also provides specialized capabilities such as fixed income, brokerage services, and treasury management solutions that help businesses optimize liquidity and cash flow.

FHN recently demonstrated strong Q4 2025 results, with adjusted net income growing by 15% YoY to $968 million. This was driven by healthy deposit base growth and high ROTCE (return on tangible common equity) of 15.0%. Notably, ROTCE accelerated during the second half of the year, exceeding the full-year ROTCE of 14.2%.

FHN’s period-end loan and deposits grew by 3% from the prior year. FHN’s rate paid on interest-bearing deposits declined by 25 basis points sequentially to 2.53%. This downward repricing on deposit rates, combined with loan growth, contributed to NII growth of $2 million on a sequential QoQ basis. As shown below, deposit growth during Q4 was driven by an inflow into indexed deposits. Indexed deposit accounts are those in which the principal is guaranteed, and interest earned is linked to the performance of a specific market index.

At the same time, FHN saw 2.6% YoY growth in its loan portfolio as of the end of 2025, driven by more loans to mortgage companies (due to higher refinancing volume). Moreover, yields on market-based commercial real estate loans for 2025 originations improved 34 basis points YoY, reflecting better pricing discipline and a favorable loan mix. As shown below, both net interest income and net interest margin expanded in the second half of 2025 due to lower deposit rates.

Credit quality remains stable, with a net charge-off percentage rate of 0.19%, which is consistent with the 0.17% to 0.22% rate over the trailing 4 quarters. At the same time, FHN’s non-performing loans declined by $5 million over the trailing 4 quarters to $604 million, and allowance for credit losses declined by 14 basis points over the same timeframe to 1.31% of the total loan book. This signals a higher management confidence in the total loan book.

Looking ahead, management is guiding for healthy 3% to 7% revenue growth during 2026. Loan growth is guided to grow in the mid-single digits, with mortgage, commercial & industrial, and commercial real estate segments as key areas of focus. This is supported by a 5% uptick in home sales during December, which was the highest in 2 years.

Recent declines in mortgage rates since Trump announced a $200 billion mortgage-buying plan strengthen the growth outlook for FHN’s loan and mortgage business. As shown below, the 30-year mortgage rate currently sits at the lowest point in over a year at 6.06% at present.

Importantly, FHN maintains a sound balance sheet with a common equity tier 1 ratio of 10.64%, which is well exceeding the 4.5% minimum requirement. FHN currently yields 2.5%, and the dividend is very well covered by a 32% payout ratio based on $1.89 in adjusted EPS for the full-year 2025.

While FHN’s dividend rate has been flat since 2020, management is returning capital to shareholders through share buybacks as well. This is reflected by $894 million in buybacks during 2025, resulting in a 3.2% reduction in the share count.

Lastly, I find value in FHN at the current price of $24.12 with a forward P/E of 11.35, sitting well below its historical P/E of 16.3, as shown below. FHN’s valuation also sits slightly below the 11.6 forward P/E of the sector median and below the 12.3 and 14.5 forward P/E of larger peers BAC and JPM.

At the current price, FHN carries an attractive 8.8% earnings yield, which it can obtain with share buybacks. The market also doesn’t seem to be giving FHN enough credit for the positive forward outlook and solid execution, particularly in Q4 and the second half of last year, as noted earlier.

Analysts estimate 5-10% annual EPS growth over the next two years. This, combined with capital returns including dividends and share buybacks as well as a potential return to mean valuation, could result in total returns in the low to mid-teens from here. Notably, SA’s Quant currently rates FHN as a ‘Strong Buy’ with a score of 4.6 out of 5.

Risks to FHN include sensitivity to interest rates. As such, declines in interest rates could lead to margin compression on loan investments if it’s unable to offset that with lower rates on deposits. Plus, while net charge-offs remain low, a deteriorating credit environment could lead to more consumer stress and a higher charge-off rate. The commercial segment could also see headwinds from tariffs, which could result in lower demand for borrowing.

Investor Takeaway

First Horizon Corp. offers an attractive mix of value, income, and disciplined execution at the current valuation. FHN has improved profitability, stable credit quality, and a solid capital position. It’s also benefiting from easing deposit costs and steady loan growth.

The stock trades at a discount to both its own history and larger peers. It also pays a well-covered dividend and is returning capital to shareholders through robust share buybacks. FHN is positioned to deliver low- to mid-teens total returns through EPS growth and capital returns, along with potential for a return to its mean valuation. Considering all the above, I rate FHN as a ‘Buy’.

data.title: Top 10 AI Stocks For 2026
data.description:
AI Demand Boom

The global artificial intelligence market is expected to soar by a CAGR of 30.6% to exceed $3 trillion by 2033, fueled by big tech AI investments, broader adoption across industries, and an explosion in data center requirements. Companies that provide AI software, hardware, and services are well-positioned to capitalize on the megatrend and deliver significant profitable growth, along with companies that are able to exploit the technology to boost capabilities, streamline operations, increase productivity, and cut costs.

AI's long-term upside may still be in its early stages. Demand for compute continues to outpace supply, constrained by infrastructure and power limitations, Blackstone said in a research report:

"AI is the most consequential force shaping the global economy today. Adoption is happening at unprecedented speed, with over 1 billion monthly active users of ChatGPT - up from fewer than 200 million just two years ago. While overall adoption has been rapid, the impact on productivity and margins is still in early stages, with far greater long-term potential. This is fueling major investment in the infrastructure that makes it all possible, from data centers and chips to power grids and connectivity."

AI stocks have outperformed the broader market and tech sector over the past year, led by chipmakers, hyperscaler suppliers, and electric vehicle manufacturers. Performance has been supported by easing interest rates and a stable economic backdrop, which have helped sustain investor appetite for growth-oriented stocks. Positive investor sentiment toward the AI theme and robust corporate earnings helped drive momentum through a brief pullback tied to valuation concerns.

Global X Artificial Intelligence & Technology ETF: 1Y Price Performance

Tech firms leading the earnings growth charge have benefited from rising prices, as demand for AI infrastructure has outpaced supply for components such as memory chips and advanced networking hardware. The same firms are projecting strong earnings growth into 2026, driven by sustained AI capex spending and improved demand visibility.

How I Picked the Top AI Stocks for 2026

SA Quant selected 10 stocks well-positioned to ride the AI boom. The stocks are pulled from the top four largest AI and Robotic ETFs by assets under management. The professional managers' vetting process helps ensure holdings are relevant, investable, and aligned with the AI theme. We then ran the stocks from the ETFs through the SA Quant system, selecting the stocks with the strongest quant ratings. This list was created by screening holdings from major AI-focused ETFs. The list includes AI chipmakers, infrastructure builders, and software providers with strong fundamentals, momentum, and earnings growth upside. The SA Quant system gathers over 100 metrics for each stock and grades them against sector peers across five factors: Growth, Profitability, Momentum, EPS Revisions, and Valuation.

1. Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE)

  • Market Capitalization: $24.34B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Communications Equipment

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 2 out of 533

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 1 out of 38

Lumentum was picked as one of my top stocks with high 2026 earnings growth targets and is a recent addition to the PRO Quant Portfolio. AI-fueled earnings growth and market-crushing momentum have made Lumentum the top quant-rated stock in the Communications Equipment industry and Technology Sector.

Lumentum makes lasers, receivers, switches, and other components for fiber-optic networks, with about 60% of revenue deriving from AI and cloud infrastructure applications. AI-powered demand for data center laser chips and optical transceivers from hyperscale customers drove revenue up by 58% in Q1 of FY 2026 to a quarterly record of $533.8M. Needham analyst Ryan Koontz cited Lumentum's investments in expanding capabilities amid the "insatiable" global demand for lasers:

"LITE is confident its aggressive capacity expansion investments will enable it to remain the largest commercial laser supplier to the cloud industry," Koontz said in a note. "Its products continue to see global shortages and the gap in 2026 appears even larger than 2025… [and] we expect Lumentum will deliver improved results across top line, margins, income, and cash flow, and shares should continue to trend higher as major segments hit stride."

Lumentum's rate of investment is reflected in a capex/sales ratio of 12%, which is impressive relative to the tech sector median of 2.3%. Surging earnings over the past year and strong forward growth undergird an A+ Growth Grade. According to consensus estimates, Lumentum earnings are expected to grow by 177% in FY26. Sell-side analysts are increasingly bullish on Lumentum's outlook, evidenced by 17 upward earnings revisions in the last 90 days to no downward revisions. LITE is set to report earnings on 2/3/2026, facing a consensus EPS growth target of +228%.

Although high earnings multiples are driving a D+ valuation grade, Lumentum's PEG of 0.99 represents a 41% discount to the sector. PEG is an important valuation metric as it combines earnings and growth. Explosive momentum, earnings growth, and earnings growth forecasts fueled by soaring laser demand make Lumentum a top AI stock for 2026.

2. Micron Technology, Inc. (MU)

  • Market Capitalization: $378.88B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Semiconductors

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 1 out of 533

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 1 out of 69

#1 on my Top 10 Stocks for 2026, Micron is a leading AI chipmaker, dominant in High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM), which is crucial for AI accelerators like NVIDIA Corporation's (NVDA) Blackwell and AMD's Instinct series. Micron has absolutely crushed the sector and industry peers over the past 12 months, showcases stellar fundamentals, and is trading at a steep discount.

Micron 1Y Price Performance vs. Industry Peers

Micron projects the HBM total addressable market to reach $100B by 2028, a CAGR of 40%, two years earlier than previous forecasts. Micron revenue surged 57% YoY to $13.6B in Q1 2026, driven by outsized AI data center memory demand from hyperscaler customers, product mix, and higher pricing. Micron has sold out of its HBM supply through 2026 and expects to set records in revenue, gross margin, earnings, and free cash flow this fiscal year. After the financial results, KeyBanc analysts said a long-term shortage in AI/data center memory is driving a "supercycle" that Micron is well-positioned to capitalize on.

Micron's pricing power has fueled surging revenue, earnings, and operating income over the past year, driving an A+ Growth Grade, bolstered by a +50% EPS forward long-term growth rate (3-5Y CAGR). Exceptional operating margins supported by higher pricing and strong cost execution reinforce an A+ Profitability Grade that includes an impressive capex/sales ratio of 42%.

EBITDA is an important metric for companies with heavy capex investments, as it offers a clearer view of core operating profitability by stripping out financing, tax, and depreciation effects. Micron's EBITDA is a robust 52% vs. a sector median of 10%. Micron has 29 upward earnings revisions in the last 3 months to no downward revisions, and FY26 EPS is projected to grow by over 290% and revenue by 100%.

Despite the surging momentum, Micron has attractive valuation multiples relative to earnings, core profitability, and cash flow, and its PEG represents an 87% discount to the sector. A member of Alpha Picks and PQP, Micron offers strong fundamentals at an attractive price for investors seeking high-quality stocks leveraged to the AI boom.

3. Ciena Corporation (CIEN)

  • Market Capitalization: $34.34B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Communications Equipment

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 5 out of 533

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 2 out of 38

Ciena Corporation is an international optical networking company with an expanding target market as AI-fueled bandwidth consumption accelerates networking requirements. As hyperscalers race to scale AI infrastructure, Ciena sits at the center of the connectivity buildout, supplying the high-speed networking equipment and fiber-optic technologies that link data centers, clusters, and global cloud footprints.

For Q4 2025, CIEN EPS of $0.91 beat by $0.14, while revenue grew 20% to $1.35B, driven by routing and switching and global services performance. Full-year revenue rose nearly 20% to $4.77B while the company generated record annual orders of $7.8B, resulting in a record backlog heading into 2026. A substantial portion of the backlog consists of cloud service providers, including hyperscalers, and CIEN sees indications that strong demand will continue into 2027 and beyond. According to consensus estimates, CIEN earnings are projected to grow by 102%, and the stock has 15 upward earnings revisions and no down revisions in the last 3 months.

CIEN has generated solid levered free cash flow in the past year, which has soared by over 48% YoY. Ciena's A- Growth Grade rests on explosive growth in earnings, revenue, and operating income, along with an EPS forward long-term growth rate of 43%. Although Ciena is trading at a high premium to the sector, its PEG ratio is at a 38% discount to the sector. Another PQP pick, Ciena is a Strong Buy stock with surging momentum and profitable growth positioned at the center of the AI infrastructure megatrend.

4. General Motors Company (GM)

  • Market Capitalization: $75.85B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Consumer Discretionary

  • Industry: Automobile Manufacturers

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 3 out of 491

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 1 out of 29

The #1 quant-rated automaker stock, GM, has made strides in leveraging AI to enhance in-vehicle connectivity, safety, and hands-free driving. GM has crushed the sector and market in price performance in the past year, getting a lift after reporting that all four of its brands recorded an annual sales increase for the second consecutive year.

GM 1Y Price Performance vs. Consumer Discretionary Sector (XLY)

GM has posted solid growth in 2025 by successfully navigating headwinds from tariffs and the expiration of EV tax credits, but the auto giant still sees electric vehicles as "the end game," even if it takes longer without the incentives. Wedbush analyst Dan Ives is bullish on GM's future growth:

"We believe Barra & Co are prepared for all options on the table as it balances its ICE and EV strategies and see this company embarking down a major path of growth ahead… with GM looking to re-calibrate around high-demand, high-margin business, we believe the company is taking the right approach to navigate the EV storm."

GM's raw cash from operations of $24.21B far exceeds the sector median for an A+ Profitability Grade. GM is set to report earnings on 1/27/2026, with Q4 2025 EPS expected to surge by 17%, and has 24 upward earnings revisions for FY25 to 1 down revision.

GM is trading at a mere 7.8x earnings with a PEG of ~0.70, marking a 57% discount to the sector, although a low dividend yield has weighed on its valuation grade. With explosive momentum and healthy cash flow generation, GM offers solid exposure to AI advancements in the auto sector.

5. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.39T

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Semiconductors

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 9 out of 533

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 2 out of 69

A major supplier to Nvidia and Apple (AAPL), TSMC dominates the global semiconductor foundry market, with its share rising from 66% to 72% through Q3 2025. Capitalizing on this momentum, the AI chipmaker's stock soared over 60% in the past year, pushing TSMC into the trillion-dollar club by mid-2025.

TSMC stock rose sharply last week after a double Q4 earnings beat fueled a global AI chip rally. TSMC reported $33.73B in quarterly revenue (+25.5%), sparked by AI-related demand for leading-edge process technologies, while EPS of $3.14 beat by $0.16. Full-year revenue grew by 35.9% to $122.42B.

Shares were also bolstered by reports that the U.S. and Taiwan are nearing a new trade deal that calls for TSMC building five more facilities in Arizona, making the company's long-term outlook even stronger. Sell-side analysts are already projecting TSMC revenue to climb from $120B in FY25 to more than $240B by FY29. Huge year-over-year growth in revenue and core profitability, along with a strong long-term EPS growth rate, drives a top-notch Growth Grade. An EBITDA margin of 68% highlights its A+ Profitability Grade, bolstered by exceptional return on equity and capital.

TSMC offers a dividend yielding about 1.00% with a 5Y growth rate of 12.3% and 21 consecutive years of payments. At roughly 23x earnings, TSMC is trading at an attractive price, and a PEG of 0.80 represents a 52% discount to the sector. A company with a dominant position on the semiconductor landscape, TSMC offers a solid opportunity for investors seeking large-cap AI exposure.

6. Hut 8 Corp. (HUT)

  • Market Capitalization: $6.19B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Application Software

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 16 out of 533

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 1 out of 176

The top quant-ranked Application Software stock, HUT, is a leading operator of Bitcoin mining and energy infrastructure operations in North America, with a development pipeline of around 12,300 MW that continues to grow. Hut's strategic pivot to an infrastructure-first model has it well-positioned for future growth, supported by pipeline expansion, a $1.6B Bitcoin reserve, and a significant stake in American Bitcoin.

Showcasing an A+ Momentum Grade, the stock surged in December after unveiling a 15-year, $7B lease with Fluidstack for 245 MW of IT capacity. StoneX Benchmark raised its price target, citing the project, which is backed by Google (GOOGL) and supports AI workloads for Anthropic:

"The transaction combined superior deal economics relative to peer deals, long-dated, investment-grade-backstopped cash flows, and multiple layers of embedded expansion optionality across three counterparties - Anthropic, Fluidstack, and Google - and we think it has positioned HUT as one of the most advantaged AI/HPC infrastructure providers in the public markets," analyst Mark Palmer wrote in a note to clients.

Hut beat Q3 2025 revenue and earnings expectations driven by rapid growth in compute-related revenue, including Bitcoin mining and AI and data center services. Hut advanced 1,530 MW of capacity from exclusivity into development during the quarter. Hut projects the portfolio has the potential to reach 2.5 GW of Energy Capacity Under Management, subject to commercialization, according to CEO Asher Genoot.

A PQP stock with blistering momentum, Hut sits at the intersection of technology and energy, backed by a deep pipeline and scalable platform positioned to benefit from rising AI power demand.

7. Celestica Inc. (CLS)

  • Market Capitalization: $36.03B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Electronic Manufacturing Services

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 14 out of 533

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 2 out of 18

Another top AI stock with A+ momentum, Celestica has surged over 2,300% over the past three years, during which the stock held a Buy or Strong Buy Quant Rating on approximately 92% of trading days. Celestica has transformed into an AI hyperscaler-integrated engineering partner, offering manufacturing and design capability advantages while riding steady demand from cloud clients.

Celestica 3Y Price Return

Earnings, operating profits, cash flow, and revenue surged in the past year for a top-notch Growth Grade, largely due to high performance from the Connectivity and Cloud Solutions segment. CLS is well-positioned to deliver more profitable growth in 2026, as U.S. hyperscale capex is expected to soar. Celestica is converting growth into returns efficiently, marked by an ROTC of 21% vs. the sector's 4% for a solid Profitability Grade. Looking ahead, Celestica's strategy is focused on early investment in design engineering and system-level solutions and shifting away from lower-margin, transactional work.

Celestica's strong execution has raised Wall Street expectations, reflected in a +58% Q4 EPS growth target as the company heads into its January 28 earnings report. Despite soaring momentum and robust growth, CLS trades at a 19% discount to the sector based on PEG - an attractive setup for an AI stock with strong fundamentals.

8. Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO)

  • Market Capitalization: $26.94B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Semiconductors

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 13 out of 533

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 3 out of 69

A provider of connectivity solutions for AI infrastructure, cloud computing, and hyperscale networks, Credo has capitalized on the expansion of AI clusters to post strong growth in its Active Electrical Cable (AEC) and integrated circuit (IC) core product offerings.

Credo reported record quarterly results in Q2 2026, with revenue up 272% to $268M, as hyperscalers ramped up orders of the company's power-efficient cables and connectivity chips. The company projects strong revenue growth and profitability through fiscal 2026 and beyond, supported by continued growth in its core AEC and IC franchises, along with upcoming ramps of recently announced connectivity platforms. The results reflect the continued build-out of the world's largest AI training and inference clusters, CEO William Brennan explained on the earnings call:

"AI clusters are no longer measured in tens of thousands of GPUs. They're now measured in hundreds of thousands and soon millions. The scale, density and complexity of these systems are pushing every aspect of interconnect. Reliability, power efficiency, signal integrity, latency, reach and total cost of ownership have all become mission-critical. This is the challenge our customers face, and it's where Credo is uniquely positioned to deliver the solutions they need to succeed."

Credo has strong core profitability and excellent forward revenue and EBITDA growth metrics. And its growth outlook has drawn strong analyst support, with sell-side estimates calling for EPS to climb nearly 300% in FY26.

Credo's valuation is high, but the PEG of 0.78 implies a 53% discount to the sector. Credo is a fast-growing semiconductor player with strong liquidity, accelerating momentum, and a solid outlook driven by AI tailwinds.

9. AppLovin Corporation (APP)

  • Market Capitalization: $205.14B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Application Software

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 20 out of 533

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 2 out of 176

A key player in the digital advertising ecosystem, AppLovin provides advanced, AI-driven marketing and monetization solutions. The stock has soared over the past year, with a boost in September after being added to the S&P 500 index, and has outperformed the tech sector, delivering solid earnings growth and a string of positive surprises.

Q3 2025 revenue rose 17% to $1.41B on continued strength in gaming advertising, adoption of its MAX platform, and earlier-than-expected international expansion. Huge operating margins and return on equity underpin APP's A+ Profitability Grade, and the robust profitability is expected to keep surging based on outstanding forward EBITDA, EPS, and revenue growth metrics. The stock is trading at a high premium, although APP's explosive long-term growth estimates have led to an attractive PEG ratio.

In starting coverage on the stock, Evercore ISI said APP is the dominant ad tech platform for mobile gaming with an emerging e-commerce performance channel that could materially expand its total addressable market. APP has ample room to grow given solid underlying industry dynamics, catalysts, and superior execution, the analysts added. APP EPS is forecast to grow by 106% in FY25 and +55% in FY26. AppLovin is turning AI-driven enhancements in ad targeting and monetization into durable revenue and profits.

10. Globus Medical, Inc. (GMED)

  • Market Capitalization: $12.57B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Health Care

  • Industry: Health Care Equipment

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 1/16/2026): 30 out of 972

Globus Medical develops medical devices focused on musculoskeletal disorders and is capitalizing on the rapid pace of AI adoption across healthcare - including software, imaging, and robotic systems - to improve precision and support complex decision-making for surgeons. According to preliminary results, GMED Q4 revenue grew 25.2% YoY to $823M, capping off a solid year that saw annual sales up 16.5% to $2.9B. The growth was driven by solid base business performance and integration of its acquisition of Nevro, a chronic pain management device specialist, CEO Keith Pfeil said in a press release:

"Fourth quarter results reflected the continued trend of growth and market share gains within US Spine, while Enabling Technologies delivered its best quarterly revenue performance to date. We look to 2026 with confidence, remaining focused on driving innovation and operational excellence, introducing a robust pipeline of new products, maintaining disciplined investment, and executing our strategy of sustained, profitable growth."

GMED has an optimistic outlook for 2026, focused on a robust pipeline of new products and executing its strategy of sustained, profitable growth. GMED's 'A' Profitability Grade rests on strong operating margins and free cash flow generation. The company also exhibits outstanding growth in earnings and return on equity, and EPS and revenue are expected to continue rising at a solid pace over the next four years. GMED has 10 upward earnings revisions in the last 90 days to zero downward revisions.

GMED showcases a PEG of 0.08, representing a staggering 88% discount to the healthcare sector, an attractive valuation supported by a pristine balance sheet and consistent growth in cash flow. GMED wraps up my top 10 AI stocks, a list of picks well positioned for leveraging the AI megatrend to deliver more growth and capital appreciation in 2026.

Top 10 Stocks Riding the AI Demand Boom in 2026

The AI market could hit $3 trillion by 2033 on big tech spending, rapid adoption, and surging data center requirements. data.title: EMB: The Final Piece Of The All Weather Pie
data.description:
This article is about the benefits of owning the iShares J.P. Morgan USD Emerging Markets Bond ETF (EMB).

I recently wrote about the benefits of owning the iShares U.S. Treasury Bond ETF (GOVT) and the iShares TIPS Bond ETF (TIP). That was because I think the Bridgewater concept of the All Weather investment strategy is an important one for investors to think about.

As Bridgewater wrote in its The All Weather Story insight note:

Any return stream can be broken down into its component parts and analyzed more accurately by first examining the drivers of those individual parts. The price of poultry depends on the price of corn and soymeal. The price of a nominal bond can be broken down into a real yield and an inflation component. A corporate bond is a nominal bond plus a credit spread. This way of thinking laid the groundwork for constructing All Weather. If assets can be broken down into different component parts and then summed up to a whole, so too could a portfolio.

The Bridgewater All Weather investment strategy requires you to have exposure to equities, commodities, corporate credit, emerging markets credit, nominal government bonds, and inflation-linked government bonds.

GOVT and TIP give you exposure to nominal and real U.S. Government Treasury returns. The iShares 10+ Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (IGLB), which I wrote about last year, covers you for corporate credit. Commodities can be accessed by the iShares S&P GSCI Commodity-Indexed Trust ETF (GSG), which I wrote about in December 2020, and in the five years since, it has beaten the return of the S&P 500 index by 16 percentage points.

Likely, you already have a portfolio of equities, and equities are the easiest asset class for most investors to execute security selection.

So the final asset class the All Weather strategy calls for is emerging market credit, as this will perform for you when we have rising growth or when we have rising inflation. Luckily, there is a highly efficient ETF for this asset class as well.

EMBI

When I worked in emerging markets bond trading 20 years ago on the sell side, our dealing universe was essentially the bonds that were in the J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index. This index covers the largest hard-currency-denominated emerging market sovereign bonds. It is the benchmark, therefore, for institutional investors who are invested in this asset class. In 2007, iShares and J.P. Morgan teamed up to create the iShares J.P. Morgan USD Emerging Markets Bond ETF. It is a very useful investment vehicle and is therefore a highly liquid ETF.

EMB

I am selecting this ETF for my DIY All Weather fund because this ETF is low-cost and liquid, and has a long-term track record and tracks what, in my mind, is a highly recognised and credible benchmark for its asset class. This ETF pays distributions monthly and at the time of writing has 674 holdings.

The ETF has an expense ratio of 0.39%, which might seem high compared to equity index trackers, but underlying emerging market bond markets are more illiquid than large-cap equities, so there's no getting around a higher expense ratio for this asset class. Still, it has a B- score from Seeking Alpha Ratings. That said, this is still cheaper than the median expense ratio of all ETFs, which is an expense ratio of 0.50%.

In fact, EMB has become such an important investment vehicle that its own Bid / Ask % Spread is just 0.01%, giving it an A+ score from Seeking Alpha ratings.

Risks

The EMB fund has an Effective Duration of 6.8 years, so I classify it in the intermediate bucket of interest rate risk. It has a three-year Equity Beta of 0.46, making it a good portfolio diversified relative to your equity holdings.

The fund has an Average Yield to Maturity of 5.93%, giving it a nice yield pick up of 203 basis points relative to GOVT. Of course, this is to compensate for the credit risk of emerging market sovereigns, relative to the United States Treasury.

The fund's holdings currently consist of 86% Sovereign bonds and 14% Agency bonds.

The iShares website provides an Excel spreadsheet with all holdings, but the top 10 specific issuing entities are as follows:

Although the reason to own this fund is to be part of an All Weather strategy, I am pleased the fund holds 3.1% in Argentina, as I am bullish on that country given President Milei's reforms there. The largest weighting, Turkey, might create some risk with The Economist saying President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan is "likely to ask parliament to call a snap election, probably in late 2027."

The fund has exposure to sovereign and/or agency bonds of all the main emerging markets. Quite literally from A to Z: Angola, Argentina, and Azerbaijan through to Zambia.

This includes a 3.3% weighting to China and 1.3% weighting to Ukraine. It is worth noting that the fund has no exposure to Russia or Venezuela.

At the end of the day, the fund's holdings are decided by what goes into the J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index, and this follows clear rules around issuer size and liquidity.

Macro

The highly diversified nature of EMB means that it really is a good vehicle to capture emerging market credit risk. Emerging market credit is generally a risk-on kind of investment, doing well when the world economy is doing well, or doing well when US interest rates come down, and investors seek higher-yielding assets, and hedge funds engage in the carry trade (borrowing at low interest rates to invest at higher interest rates).

I don't have a strong view on near or medium-term emerging market credit spreads, although if you put a gun to my head, I would say I think the Federal Reserve will be dovish, and this normally supports emerging markets.

More importantly, EMB is an excellent way to allocate to the EM credit bucket in the All Weather investment strategy, given its broad exposure to the asset class, relatively low cost, and high trading liquidity. That liquidity might be reduced at the next "emerging markets crisis", which will come around sooner or later, but we are not owning this investment as a trade, but as a long-term investment.

Conclusion

I, like you, want to pick stocks for my equity allocation. For the other All Weather asset class exposures, I recommend buy and hold positions in GSG for commodities, IGLB for corporate credit, GOVT for nominal government bonds, TIP for inflation-linked government bonds, and EMB for emerging markets credit.

data.title: Aluminium And Copper: A Rally Fuelled By Structural Strains And Political Uncertainty
data.description:
By Erwan Jacob, Macro Analyst, Datastream & Macroeconomics at LSEG

Aluminium and copper prices have seen notable gains, reflecting a mix of supply constraints and policy developments. While short-term volatility remains, structural factors and geopolitical shifts continue to influence market dynamics. Understanding these drivers is key to assessing the outlook for 2026:

  • Aluminium strength – Prices supported by production caps, environmental standards, and changes in China’s trade position.
  • Copper challenges – Supply disruptions and tariff uncertainty add pressure to an already fragile market.
  • Demand outlook – Energy transition and industrial applications underpin steady long-term demand for both metals.

Aluminium price surge and trade policy developments

Aluminium recently hit a three-year high, hovering around $2,900 per tonne. Other base metals, including zinc, lead, and tin, also recorded significant gains. Several factors are driving this upward trend for aluminium. Chiefly, US-China trade talks have progressed, with the US announcing NVIDIA’s most advanced chips will remain eligible for export to China. In addition, China is also set to suspend export controls on rare earth metals and end investigations into US chipmakers. Meanwhile, the US will pause some of the current administration "reciprocal tariffs" on China for another year and halt plans to implement a 100% tariff on Chinese exports that were set to take effect last year.

Geopolitical dynamics and China’s evolving role

Geopolitical dynamics are also shaping aluminium prices beyond tariff challenges. A closer Russia-China relationship is visible through export and import of raw materials and commodities – including aluminium. China has increased its aluminium imports from Russia – almost doubling year-on-year. At the same time, China’s role in global aluminium markets is shifting. Exports of semi-finished aluminium products have declined, while imports of primary aluminium have risen. These changes suggest a fundamental change in China's position within global aluminium markets – transforming from a net exporter to potentially a net importer.

Figure 1: China (in Thousands USD) vs US Aluminium Exports (in USD Millions)

Production caps and environmental standards

Another key factor of the increase in price is constrained supply, as China maintains a strict cap on aluminium production. The limit – set a 45 million tons of primary aluminium annually – was introduced to address overcapacity and environmental concerns. The Chinese aluminium sector has gone through significant growth, expanding from 4 million tons in 2002 to over 43 million tons in 2024, capturing approximately 60% of global production.

Figure 2: Aluminium Price and Warehouse Stocks

China’s environmental standards for aluminium production include a maximum energy consumption of 13,000 kWh per ton, stricter emissions controls, and a ban on new projects in heavily polluted regions. The aluminium industry is energy-intensive, with smelting operations traditionally relying on coal power. China's production limits aim to reduce carbon emissions by up to 65 million tons annually and support China's carbon neutrality goals for 2060. These environmental considerations have become increasingly important as China aims to balance industrial growth with ecological sustainability.

Figure 3: China's industrial output in tens of thousands and YoY evolution

Structural demand drivers for aluminium

Beyond the geopolitical context and production caps, structural trends point to sustained growth in aluminium demand. The International Aluminium Institute (IAI) expects aluminium demand to rise by 40% by 2030, due to the following factors:

  • Electric vehicle production – Aluminium is essential for battery housing and chassis components.
  • Renewable energy – Solar panels, wind turbines, and energy storage systems rely on aluminium for its corrosion resistance and durability.
  • AI-driven data centres – The surge in data centre construction for AI has added unexpected demand pressure, as the metal's thermal properties make it essential for cooling systems and electrical infrastructure.

Copper market trends and price outlook

Copper prices in London hit a new high in 2025, with 3-month London Metal Exchange (LME) futures trading above $11,400 per ton. Ongoing supply disruptions have driven this surge, which is expected to persist in 2026. Estimates now indicate a 2.2% production growth for refined copper in 2026, down from previous forecasts of 2.8% growth. Average copper ore grades at major operations continue declining at 2-3% annually, forcing mining companies to process substantially larger volumes of lower-grade material to maintain equivalent copper output.

Operational disruptions and supply fragility

Operational disruptions have significantly impacted Copper production and prices. In 2025 – Grasberg – the world's second-biggest copper mine, in Indonesia – suspended operations after mud flows flooded the site, resulting in severe safety incidents. In the Congo, a tragic event at Kalando mine in Lualaba province led to multiple casualties. Additional pressure may stem from delays at Teck’s Quebrada Blanca II project in Chile and the indefinite suspension of First Quantum’s Cobre Panamá. Slow output recovery in Chile, and recurring community protests in Peru have exacerbated the structural fragility of global copper mine supply, heightening risks of prolonged price volatility.

Figure 4: US COMEX vs London LME Copper Prices

Tariff uncertainty and inventory strategies

Copper pricing is caught between the production challenges and the unexpected tariff announcements, which have disrupted the term structure of premiums and influenced inventory strategies. Early last year, President Trump announced that tariffs will be applied on copper imports into the US market. However, by late July, the plan shifted: levies would apply only to value-added copper products, with potential tariffs on commodity-grade forms postponed until 2027 onwards.

US importers have accelerated copper orders as tariff uncertainty weighs on inventories and on pricing. Indeed, regardless of consumption patterns, prices are increasingly influenced by perceived scarcity and the risk of steep duties – potentially as high as 50% on refined copper imports. Our latest in-house forecasts now include scenarios exhibiting a production shortage of 40,000 tonnes in 2026, surpassing previous estimates and underscoring the intensifying supply-demand imbalance. These projections incorporate mine disruption frequency, Chinese smelter capacity constraints, and shifts in global import flows.

Figure 5: US COMEX vs London LME Warehouses Copper stock trends

Arbitrage and location-based pricing dynamics

The increased risk of tariffs and production challenges has altered pricing upon trading location. The arbitrage between London and New York grew in 2025 to approximately $400 per ton – far above the typical $50-100 range – before retreating. Concurrently, US-bound shipments reached record quarterly volumes above 500,000 tons. Trading houses report that shipping costs, insurance, and financing remain profitable even at current premium levels. This suggests that the differential may persist as policy uncertainty continues.

Critically low inventories and liquidity risks

Exchange-visible copper inventories have declined to critically low levels, amplifying price volatility and reducing market liquidity during supply disruptions. Historically, when inventory has declined below 200,000 tonnes, supply chain challenges intensify, location-based price differentials widen, and volatility spikes. The situation becomes more concerning given that a large portion of exchange-held copper is tied up as collateral for financing or committed to delivery contracts. As a result, this reduces the volume available for immediate physical settlement.

2026 outlook: Resilient demand amid policy shifts

In summary, the demand for aluminium and copper is expected to remain resilient in 2026, even as China faces economic challenges such as deflation risk and subdued domestic consumption. Long-term structural drivers – such as decarbonisation and the global energy transition – will continue to exert upward pressure on prices. However, reduced volatility around trade policies may lessen the incentive to build inventories, helping to stabilise price disruption.

data.title: Mairs & Power Balanced Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Artificial intelligence (AI) and increasing market concentration took center stage again in 2025. The rate of investment in technology and AI infrastructure spending is driving the market narrative with valuations pushing higher, corporate spending accelerating, and bubble-like behaviors percolating beneath the surface.

Despite the dizzying activity, there were also encouraging signs with cooling inflation, a Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cut in the fourth quarter, and continued resilience in corporate earnings. The consistency of corporate earnings is a major reason for the continued strength of the stock market. The S&P 500 is projected to deliver 12% earnings growth in 2025, which is below the beginning of the year forecast for 15% growth, but still a decent result in the face of significant policy uncertainty. Additionally, small cap companies are showing their first signs of earnings growth recovery after three years of contraction, posting 13% growth in 2025. Still, the gap between price and earnings remains wide in many places. While AI-related companies continue to command premium valuations, other sectors, particularly defensives, mid-caps, and many industrial names, remain reasonably priced. This valuation divide continues to guide much of our investment activity.

On the economic front, inflation continued to ease across the CPI, PPI, and PCE measures, landing closer to the Fed’s long-term target. Though still imperfect, these readings gave policymakers confidence to begin cutting rates in the fourth quarter, albeit with dissenting opinions. While the labor market cooled in 2025, it remains on solid footing with unemployment still below five percent.

While equity markets marched toward new highs, U.S. consumers felt mounting pressure. Rising costs, including insurance, utilities, property taxes, and groceries, continued to weigh heavily on households. In response, the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index fell to levels typically associated with recessionary conditions. All these data points highlight a disconnect between Wall Street optimism and Main Street anxiety.

Looking at the indices for the fourth quarter, the S&P 500 Total Return (TR) was 2.66% for the quarter and returned 17.88% in 2025. Meanwhile, the Russell 2000 (TR) was 2.19% in the quarter and 12.81% for the year, and the Bloomberg U.S. Government/Credit Bond Index was 0.90% in the fourth quarter and 6.88% for the year.

Future Outlook

A recent McKinsey report titled, “The cost of compute: A $7 trillion race to scale data centers,” projects that nearly $7 trillion in capital expenditures will be needed worldwide by 2030 to build up the infrastructure needed to keep up with AI demand. For comparison, the cost of building out cloud computing infrastructure is estimated to be around $500 billion. One of the closest comparisons to what McKinsey is estimating would be roughly $5 trillion spent during the industrial boom, but that process took decades.

While we can’t predict all that the new year will hold for the markets, we do believe we are entering a transition period for AI. As we’ve seen with previous periods of rapid technological innovation, the initial excitement of the building-out phase is often short-lived and filled with promises (and companies) that don’t ultimately live up to expectations. Given the flood of capital into AI and the increasing use of debt and unusual financing structures, there are signals that the cycle is moving into a higher risk phase.

A helpful data point as we look at 2026 and beyond is the expectation for lower interest rates. That process began in 2024, but accelerated in the second half of 2025, and is expected to further cool in 2026. Interest rate changes, up or down, take time to work their way through the economy, but it typically takes around a year to feel the effects from changes. Also, though there has been an uptick in layoffs at large companies, it is important to remember that small businesses are the real job engine in the economy, accounting for over 80% of employment. Lower rates, and more importantly, rising small business profits, would likely support an uptick in hiring and, in turn, consumer confidence.

Finally, more stability in terms of trade and economic policy could also go a long way toward improving the consumer outlook. Thus far tariffs have not proved to be the economic disaster many predicted, largely since they have not been fully implemented. While the legal status of tariffs remains in limbo, a more stable environment would benefit confidence and, in turn, hiring and spending.

Our approach to navigating this environment remains firmly centered on identifying businesses that will harness technological advancement (e.g., AI) over the long-term, not just during the initial build-out, which we believe can increase those companies’ competitive advantages and, consequently, their growth rates. While this can mean missing out on some of the early “excitement,” identifying those companies benefiting most from these secular shifts is consistent with our philosophy and process, and we believe it is a prudent strategy for both compounding and protecting wealth over the long term.

Executive Summary

  • During 2025, the Fund underperformed its benchmark by 6.91% due to equity underperformance.
  • The most significant contributor to equity underperformance came from the Information Technology sector where unfavorable selection and a relative underweight allocation to a small handful of leading AI-oriented securities weighed on performance. In addition, Fiserv (FISV) and UnitedHealth Group (UNH) were significant detractors from performance as both companies experienced execution and management issues during the year after being long-term success stories for the Fund.
  • Roche (RHHBY) contributed positively to performance due to favorable developments in its new drug pipeline. JP Morgan Chase (JPM) also contributed positively, driven by improving loan volumes, a favorable interest rate environment, and generally stable credit conditions.
  • During 2025, the Fund added new positions in WEC Energy Group (WEC), Amazon (AMZN), and Travelers (TRV) while it trimmed existing positions in Alphabet (GOOG) and US Bank (USB). The Fund exited its positions in Honeywell (HON), Principal Financial Group (PFG), Neogen (NEOG), and Target (TGT).
  • Fixed income outperformed on credit selection and shorter maturities in the Fund which benefited from curve steepening (short-term rates down, long-term rates up).

Performance Review

The Mairs & Power Balanced Fund finished the year up 6.61%. The Fund underperformed both the benchmark composite index (60% S&P 500 Total Return Index and 40% Bloomberg U.S. Government/Credit Bond Index), which was up 13.52%, and the Morningstar Moderate Allocation peer group, which rose 12.90%.

Performance data quoted represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance of the Fund may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. For the most recent month-end performance figures, please call Shareholder Services at (800) 304-7404. Expense Ratio 0.71%.

While the Fund’s absolute performance was nearly in-line with its 20-year average, relative performance lagged the benchmark due to underperformance from the equity portion of the portfolio. The Fund’s bond portfolio produced positive total returns and outperformed the asset class benchmark.

The Information Technology sector was the largest contributor to the underperformance of equities during 2025. A small handful of companies witnessed outsized share price performance driven by enthusiasm around AI and the Fund did not have sufficient exposure to this cohort. Additionally, several technology companies the Fund has exposure to also underperformed the market. For Entegris (ENTG), margin pressure from expansionary efforts, combined with lackluster demand, helped contribute to lower earnings expectations. For Motorola Solutions (MSI), underlying results remain quite positive, but a recent acquisition is expected to dilute earnings at a time when tariff headwinds are expected to create near-term margin pressure. For Texas Instruments (TXN), the company continues to make significant long-term investments to expand capacity but in the near term has been hampered by a weak demand environment.

The Financials sector was also a significant contributor to relative underperformance due to adverse stock selection. Fiserv was a significant holding which dramatically underperformed after revising down its earnings guidance two times during the year. These revisions were due to disappointing results for its Clover product, a cloud-based payment platform which had previously been identified as a significant driver of growth in merchant acceptance. Unexpected market share losses led management to announce a strategic pivot which will result in lower near-term earnings. Offsetting the Fiserv headwind, JPMorgan & Chase (JPM) and Wells Fargo (WFC) outperformed due to an improving outlook for credit conditions as well as a trend toward deregulation which we believe will benefit the largest banks. Wells Fargo further benefited as several consent decrees, which outline specific reforms and improvements, were lifted by various regulators. With these restrictions aside, the company is now in a much better position for future growth. Separately, the Fund trimmed its holdings in U.S. Bancorp (USB) due to a declining competitive advantage position which could weigh on long-term growth expectations.

The Health Care sector also weighed on relative performance due to adverse stock selection. UnitedHealth Group (UNH) lagged during the period as the company experienced higher than expected medical loss ratios which led to a significant decline in earnings expectations. Consequently, the company announced that former CEO Stephen Hemsley would return to the top role to “steady the ship.” The company has since laid out some credible steps toward improvement such as “exiting” 1 million unprofitable Medicare Advantage members while also adjusting premiums to reflect higher costs. The company still has an attractive long-term earnings profile and historically has demonstrated its ability to manage through temporary disturbances. We continue to hold our UnitedHealth position and may look to add when valuation opportunities present themselves. Bio-Techne (TECH), which makes high quality ingredients for biopharmaceutical research and production, underperformed due to uncertainty around the funding environment for biopharmaceuticals and biotech research given recent federal funding cuts and weakness in venture capital funding for biotech startups. We have taken advantage of the recent weakness to add to our Bio- Techne position. Offsetting these impacts, Eli Lilly (LLY) outperformed due to an improving outlook for its market-leading GLP-1 products while Roche (RHHBY) outperformed due to robust potential in its new product pipeline where 10 new products advanced to phase III, one step away from FDA approval.

The Consumer Discretionary sector contributed positively to relative returns. The Fund initiated a new position in Amazon (AMZN) in the spring during the market selloff associated with tariff announcements and benefited from the subsequent share price recovery. This benefit was partly offset by lagging performance from Home Depot (HD) which lagged due to tariff headwinds and sluggish housing activity.

Relative performance benefited from our lack of exposure to the Energy and Real Estate sectors, both of which lagged the market during 2025.

In the fourth quarter, the Fund initiated a position in Travelers (TRV), once a long-time holding of the Fund, as the company has proven its data and operational excellence has helped it develop a long-term competitive advantage in the insurance carrier space. The company has navigated several difficult elements in the past five years, and its improving profitability profile led to our confidence in re-establishing a position. Earlier in the year, the Fund added WEC Energy (WEC) as a high-quality utility with improving growth prospects and a best-in-class regulator.

Over the course of 2025, treasury interest rates out to 10 years fell meaningfully while 20–30-year interest rates largely stayed the same. Fixed income holdings in the Fund outperformed the index in 2025 as there are very few longer-term securities held in the Fund and the preference for corporate bonds and credit selection benefited performance. At the end of the year, several measures of interest rate volatility suggest a more muted tone in the future. The markets are notoriously horrible at predicting the future, and we give little credence to these projections, but for the sake of everyone, we hope for the lower volatility future.

Credit spreads, which were reflective of a benign credit environment at the beginning of the year, expanded with the market selloff in April but quickly retreated at the end of June and have since hovered near the lowest levels of this millennia. We have found fewer opportunities for investment lately and have generally been raising credit quality in the portfolio in small increments, most recently trimming some of our preferred bank securities which are sensitive to the credit cycle. We will continue to execute our strategy in fixed income, whereby we seek pockets of value and selectively research and buy bonds to help us achieve long-term performance.

Our equity investment process leads us to select companies with durable competitive advantages and attractive growth prospects which we believe will lead to sustainable long-term investment returns. We will use market volatility to invest at reasonable valuation levels as we did to initiate a position in Amazon. In an environment of shifting geopolitical dynamics, we have a general preference for domestic equities and domestic fixed income which we believe leaves us less exposed to emerging risks. We are, and will be, long-term believers in the United States economy and our country’s innovative prowess. While we do not make big calls on interest rates or credit cycles, we have increasingly positioned the fixed income portfolio to be optimized for a balanced portfolio allowing for effective reallocation in a market disruption. We continue to believe that our consistent investment approach to both stocks and bonds will deliver sustainable performance over the long haul.

data.title: CQQQ: A Strong Run, But No Case For Fresh Money
data.description:
The Invesco China Technology ETF (CQQQ) has had a stellar run in the past year but has not been a consistent compounder like QQQ over more than a decade. It remains sensitive to policy risks and misses out on gamechangers like Alibaba and Huawei. What is left is broadly a portfolio of consumer-facing tech platforms that are cyclical in nature. With no secular rerating trigger in sight for the top holdings and a neutral broad market setup, CQQQ does not look good for fresh longs, both for long-term investors and those looking for tactical positions.

Methodology and Implications

CQQQ is a rules-based China tech ETF that includes broad structural exposure not restricted to offshore tech names like Tencent. The market cap range is quite broad, and final weights are assigned using a modified market cap-weighted methodology, implying big names will dominate. There is no emphasis on factors, and it is best viewed as a high beta proxy of the overall Chinese tech segment (akin to QQQ for the US)—but more policy and sentiment driven.

Positions in CQQQ are usually a bet on China tech multiples expanding, accompanied by a regime where policy pressures are low and overall Chinese markets are flush with liquidity and risk appetite. On the other hand, CQQQ is expected to underperform when regulatory uncertainty rises or when earnings visibility is not strong. CQQQ (like QQQ) may work in markets where leadership is narrow but needs an overall bullish local tech environment to truly flourish.

Portfolio Walkthrough

As discussed before, CQQQ is not a tech-only portfolio from a purist's standpoint. Including communications, the exposure is still the vast majority of the portfolio (~83%). However, a significant proportion of the tech names in the portfolio include consumer-facing and platform-driven companies (not enterprise software, cloud infra, or B2B semis like in the US). As a result, returns are driven by consumer demand and engagement levels rather than by variables that influence QQQ, such as enterprise IT budgets or SaaS pricing power.

The holdings are concentrated at the top because of the market cap-based methodology. Although the portfolio reports more than 150 holdings, the top 10 account for ~55% of the portfolio by weight. Much like Nvidia in QQQ, Tencent is the real anchor with a close to a 10% weight in the portfolio. But unlike Nvidia, this is a social platform-based model with no secular tailwind. That business model difference is the flavor broadly throughout the portfolio, making CQQQ far more cyclical than QQQ, which is driven by visible earnings growth and secular tailwinds (like AI and global base). The total exposure to AI/semis in the top 10 holdings, even when the definition is broadened, amounts to just double digits (including Hua Hong, Horizon, and Sensetime). Bigger influential names like Huawei remain private—any AI tailwind there and in similar companies is not captured in CQQQ. What remains in CQQQ's tech portfolio is, on the other hand, more communications and services-oriented areas that are heavily regulated and politically sensitive. On top of missing out on private names, the ETF's tech classification net misses a strong name like Alibaba.

Use Cases

CQQQ's positioning makes it unsuitable as a core EM holding (cyclical and policy risks minus global secular tailwinds like AI) or a defensive China exposure, in my opinion. Another use case that naturally arises by definition is CQQQ as a complementary holding to QQQ, acting either as a hedge or a long-term growth enhancer.

However, the tech allocations tilt—internet platforms versus enterprise software/infrastructure—make it not a real apples-to-apples complement. CQQQ lacks an innovation-led growth in the long term and does not necessarily hedge tech drawdowns in the US because it focuses mostly on localized markets and remains sensitive to its own idiosyncrasies. Incidental periods of upside while US tech cools can happen, but there is no logical thread tying such expectations together, nor a common market geographically or by segment.

Since 2010, QQQ has grown by over 1500%, while CQQQ has grown by only 166% and through cycles. The return enhancer role is not a long-term proposition; simply looking at the historical gap in performance can best be used tactically when the outlook is supportive. For a position in CQQ (as a complement or simply rotation), investors need to have an outlook where US tech leadership weakens structurally, and China tech continues monetization highs.

The hedging or diversification angle is still in play to an extent. However, here too, correlation is positive and mostly persistent. There are very few regimes when the correlation turns negative and stays negative consistently—even accounting for what are more likely market regimes where US tech rallied, and CQQQ probably corrected (visual inspection of charts below).

Current Outlook

Since CQQQ does not seem to have a long-term strategic role to play in US tech portfolios, its tactical use case today hinges purely on the outlook for China tech (and broad macro overall). CQQQ has had a bull run in the past year with stellar returns of ~42%. The liquidity situation has been supportive. Forex reserves have been steadily improving in 2025. However, this is not as strong a long-duration support from here on, as earnings or consumer strength-led rallies, particularly given foreign direct investments (FDI) are actually sliding. FDI in 2025 (until November) was reported down by 7.5% despite incentives to attract capital. Globally, funds are still flowing into the US compared to EM or Asian counterparts. Until a sustained global interest is observed in Chinese tech and broader equities, I prefer to remain on the sidelines—especially after a strong 2025 for CQQQ.

When it comes to valuations, apart from Baidu, the other two material holdings—Tencent and PDD—do not show any broad expansion trends to go after. The good that emerges from valuations of the top 3 holdings overall is that valuations are not expensive broadly, but no secular valuation expansion trend is observed across the board to chase. The Shanghai Information & Technology sector P/E was about ~34x in late 2025, down from the ~50x highs post-COVID. This reduces drawdown risks and certainly shows tech stocks are not overheated - but also shows a lack of strong momentum that can be capitalized on. Most of the rally seen in the past year appears to be around earnings revisions after they are evident, rather than a regime where price leads earnings (like we are seeing in the US tech space). When rallies lead earnings, future growth becomes more credible in my view. The upside is therefore mostly mechanical in CQQQ, not convex, as earnings simply translate to linear share price returns.

The final variable of interest, policy risk, appears to also be neutral. The overhang of curbs on social platforms is still real in China, although the rally in the past year points indirectly to a regime where policy risks have not dragged growth.

Not a Buy Now

There are two specific times when CQQQ becomes a Buy in my view. One, a period of corrections taking CQQQ down significantly from the last highs—CQQQ's past cyclical behavior points to that being the best time to enter. We had that opportunity up until the start of 2025, when geopolitical risks and uncertainties started impacting China as much as the US. CQQQ has rallied sharply since. There could still be steam left in the rally, but a Buy now needs extra bullish confirmation now than in the 2022-24 lows.

In terms of confirmation, we have none. All we have is a neutral setup in terms of liquidity and valuation expansion momentum. Breaking down the portfolio, the overall upside in most holdings also supports my cautionary view for fresh longs. Tencent looks well poised to deliver stable earnings and cash generation, but no rerating trigger is in sight. PDD's US exposure in e-commerce is fraught with geopolitical uncertainties. Baidu looks the most promising overweight in the CQQQ portfolio, but a lot of rerating is already behind us.

Overall, in the absence of broad market rerating triggers, stock-specific action appears to be of limited impact for a high conviction Buy at this stage. Therefore, CQQQ is a hold—both for long-term investors (because of its cyclical trends) and also for tactical allocations (good run-up recently without backing foreign investments interest or broad valuation expansion).

data.title: Netflix Earnings Preview: Q4 2025
data.description:
The preview

While the market awaits the outcome of a possible Warner Brothers (WBD) acquisition, the shares are likely to remain sensitive to the news flow around a potential deal. The fight for great content and audiences by both Netflix (NFLX) and Paramount is likely an indication of YouTube’s continued growth and strength. With AI innovations coming and content creators continuing to gain viewers globally, legacy studio and media franchises may need to reimagine their business models to stay relevant. The investment of $200 million by famed investor, Tom Lee, to Beast Industries seems to be an early indication of where the future of video content is headed. YouTube channel, Mr. Beast, currently has over 460 million YouTube subscribers.

Expectations for Netflix Inc. (NFLX) into Q4 have remained stable since July 2025. Last quarter, the company highlighted the slate for Q4, but it was not a catalyst for revenue expectations. Visible Alpha consensus is expecting $12.0 billion in revenue for Q4 and $45.1 billion for FY 2025. Revenues are expected to be supported by continued new membership and monetization. Offering a range of pricing and plans combined with continuing growth in the ads business is expected to further increase monetization. Consensus expects the operating margin to increase to 24.1% in Q4 and to 29.6% for FY 2025, down 70 bps from previous expectations of 30.3% due to assumptions about higher expenses.

The company expects to grow revenues by increasing engagement trends and reducing churn while offering a more diverse entertainment offering. Gaming and the growth of ads could be key drivers in 2026. According to consensus, analysts now expect the company to generate a 32.7% margin, on expected revenue of $51.0 billion and $16.7 billion in operating profit in FY 2026.

In the spring quarter, Management highlighted that the Ad Tier enables lower prices. The Company stated in the earnings call that they expect that ads revenue will roughly double year-over-year again in FY 2025. Q4 2025 expectations for the ad-supported revenues have nearly doubled since July, suggesting increasing optimism in this emerging area of revenue generation. FY 2025 expectations have moved higher from the initial forecast of $4.0 billion back in early 2024, the expected trend has improved and is now at $5.9 billion for the full year.

Netflix remains upbeat about the long-term opportunity, given the size of its user base. Co-CEO Gregory Peters explained that 2025 will be the year that the ads business will step up to the plate to swing. Currently, consensus projects total ad-supported revenue for FY 2026 to expand to $8.7 billion, up over $2.5 billion from the expected $6.2 billion last quarter. There is a significant range of views on the magnitude of impact the ad-supported revenues will have on operating profit growth. For FY 2027, the analyst estimates for ad-supported revenue further increased to over $3 billion to $11.5 billion, but the operating profit has remained stable at $19.9 billion. The management commentary on the potential for operating profit and margin growth from the ad-supported revenues will be an important dimension to the FY 2026 outlook for the business.

Based on Visible Alpha consensus, the operating profit margin is expected to grow from 26.7% in FY 2024 to 35.0% in FY 2027. Currently, consensus estimates the operating margin now to exceed 32.7% by the end of FY 2026. However, there is significant debate among analysts with respect to FY 2026 margin estimates, which range from 31% to 35%. The debate in the market seems to be around how quickly the Company will achieve a 35% operating profit margin. This margin growth is expected to take FY 2027 expected diluted EPS from $3.94/share or 22x FY 2027 P/E, significantly lower than the 30x it traded in summer. The current consensus target price has ticked down slightly to $130 or 48% upside from the current levels.

This article was published by Visible Alpha, part of S&P Global Market Intelligence and not by S&P Global Ratings, which is a separately managed division of S&P Global.
